channel.title: NYT > Top Stories
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: War and Energy Shortages Boost China’s Influence in Asia
item.description: The war in Iran has left China’s neighbors appealing for help, handing Beijing the kind of sway it has long sought.
item.dc:creator: Alexandra Stevenson and Murphy Zhao
item.category: Far East, South and Southeast Asia and Pacific Areas
US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Oil storage tanks at a depot in Hong Kong. Shipments of Chinese oil products to select countries have offered relief during the war and underscored how Beijing engenders good will in times of crisis, experts say.

item.title: Surging Gas Prices Shake Americans, and How Ted Turner Unleashed 24-Hour News
item.description: Plus, the conflicting messages on the state of the war.
item.dc:creator: Tracy Mumford, Will Jarvis, Margaret Kadifa, Jake Lucas, Ian Stewart, Jim Rutenberg and Talmon Joseph Smith
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Surging gas prices are inflaming a longstanding economic divide in America, as households with lower incomes struggle to pay more at the pump amid already elevated prices.

item.title: 5 Takeaways From the California Governor Debate
item.description: Candidates debated housing and insurance policy in the first half, then furiously attacked one another at the end.
item.dc:creator: Soumya Karlamangla and Laurel Rosenhall
item.media:description: Seven candidates competing to be the next governor of California met for a debate at the Skirball Cultural Center in Los Angeles on Wednesday.

item.title: Susan Collins Says She Has Long Had a Benign Tremor
item.description: The Republican senator from Maine, running for re-election at age 73 in one of this year’s top Senate races, made the disclosure after mounting online scrutiny on the left.
item.dc:creator: Katie Glueck
item.media:description: Senator Susan Collins of Maine, a Republican, is likely to face Graham Platner, a 41-year-old Democrat and a political newcomer, in the November general election.

item.title: Judge Says F.B.I. Can Keep 2020 Election Records Seized From Georgia
item.description: The ruling is the latest in a saga driven by President Trump’s desire for redemption in the state, which he lost in 2020. The county is likely to appeal.
item.dc:creator: Tim Balk and Richard Fausset
item.media:credit: Nicole Craine for The New York Times
item.media:description: The F.B.I. searched an election center in Fulton County, Ga., in January.

item.title: Jeffrey Epstein’s Purported Suicide Note Is Released by Federal Judge
item.description: A federal judge released the note, which Jeffrey Epstein’s former cellmate said he found in a graphic novel. The New York Times has not authenticated that Mr. Epstein wrote it.
item.dc:creator: Benjamin Weiser, Jan Ransom and Steve Eder

item.title: Florida Creates a More Conservative Course to Rival A.P. History
item.description: Here are ways Florida’s advanced high school history program, which students can use for college credit, differs from other curriculums.
item.dc:creator: Dana Goldstein
item.media:credit: Nicole Craine for The New York Times
item.media:description: Gov. Ron DeSantis has pushed forward a more conservative agenda for Florida’s schools, including through criticism of Advanced Placement courses.

item.title: Retracing Henry Knox’s Revolutionary War Trek Across the Wilderness
item.description: In 1775, George Washington sent Henry Knox, a Boston bookseller, to fetch 60 tons of artillery. We followed Knox’s route to see how the country has changed.
item.dc:creator: Richard Rubin and Tony Cenicola
item.media:description: Henry Knox and his Noble Train of Artillery crossed 300 miles of snowy wilderness and icy lakes between northern New York and Cambridge, Mass., to supply George Washington with weapons.

item.title: Jack Smith Calls the Justice Dept. ‘Corrupted’ by Trump and His Allies
item.description: At a private event in Washington last month, Mr. Smith, the former special counsel, accused Justice Department leaders of targeting people for prosecution to please and impress the president.
item.dc:creator: Glenn Thrush
item.category: Federal Criminal Case Against Trump (Documents Case)
Federal Criminal Case Against Trump (2020 Election Case)
item.media:description: Jack Smith, the special counsel who twice indicted President Trump, testifying at a hearing in January in Washington.

item.title: Netherlands Tests Flight Attendant for Hantavirus as Officials Track Cruise Ship Outbreak
item.description: Global health officials are tracking people connected to a deadly outbreak on a cruise ship. One infected passenger had briefly boarded a flight from South Africa to the Netherlands before she died.
item.dc:creator: Claire Moses
item.media:description: An ambulance during an evacuation of suspected hantavirus patients, in Praia, Cape Verde, on Wednesday.

item.title: U.S. Health Authorities Monitor Hantavirus Cruise Passengers in Georgia, California and Arizona
item.description: People in at least three states are being monitored after being on the ship. None have shown symptoms, officials said.
item.dc:creator: Rylee Kirk and Nina Agrawal
item.category: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention
item.media:description: Three sick people aboard the MV Hondius were evacuated on Wednesday.

item.title: Should You Worry About Hantavirus?
item.description: Here’s what to know about the virus, how it spreads and the risk to the general public.
item.dc:creator: Nina Agrawal and Dani Blum

item.title: Ted Turner’s Legacy as a Landowner and Conservationist
item.description: The media mogul, who died this week, amassed roughly two million acres and revived entire ecosystems.
item.dc:creator: Ronda Kaysen, Kevin Draper and Jack Healy
item.media:credit: Linda Best/Bozeman Daily Chronicle, Associated Press
item.media:description: Ted Turner, a media giant, was also a visionary landowner, reshaping American conservation.

item.title: Ted Turner’s Mark on Atlanta Lived On, Even After He Left the Spotlight
item.description: The city where he planted his empire came to embody his ambitions and his confidence. Mr. Turner died on Wednesday at 87.
item.dc:creator: Rick Rojas
item.media:description: Ted Turner shows off his World Series Championship ring before the Atlanta Braves’ season-opening game in 1995.

item.title: Starmer Braces for Local Election Losses Amid New Era of UK Politics
item.description: Polls predict historic losses for Prime Minister Keir Starmer’s Labour Party on Thursday as anti-immigrant Reform U.K. makes gains, and a new era of multiparty politics takes shape.
item.dc:creator: Michael D. Shear and Stephen Castle
item.media:credit: Andrew Testa for The New York Times
item.media:description: A mural showing Aneurin Bevan, the Labour Party hero who helped create the U.K.’s National Health Service, in Tredegar, Wales.

item.title: The ‘Perfect Storm’ Hanging Over Britain’s Public Debt
item.description: Bond investors are expected to scrutinize the results of critical local elections on Thursday as the war in the Middle East adds inflation pressure on the government.
item.dc:creator: Eshe Nelson
item.media:credit: Joseph Horton for The New York Times
item.media:description: Investors are betting the Bank of England will approve several interest rate increases this year, reversing previous expectations that rates would come down.

item.title: What Are ‘Teen Takeovers’ and Why Are Police Struggling to Stop Them?
item.description: Across the country, police and city officials are trying to crack down on sometimes violent youth gatherings, but the teens themselves say they need some way to socialize and blow off steam.
item.dc:creator: Clyde McGrady, Emma Schartz and Julie Bosman
item.media:credit: Jamie Kelter Davis for The New York Times
item.media:description: Police officers detained a young person after chasing him down a street amid rumors of a “teen takeover” in the Hyde Park neighborhood of Chicago last month.

item.title: New York’s $268 Billion Budget Deal Includes New Second-Home Tax
item.description: Gov. Kathy Hochul announced a handshake budget deal with state lawmakers that also seeks to push back on federal immigration enforcement in the state.
item.dc:creator: Grace Ashford and Benjamin Oreskes
item.media:credit: Mimi d’Autremont for The New York Times
item.media:description: The state budget agreement announced by Gov. Kathy Hochul on Thursday came more than five weeks after the April 1 deadline.

item.title: ICE Agents Barred From Wearing Masks in New York Under State Budget Deal
item.description: State and local officials will also be prohibited from formally cooperating with Immigrations and Customs Enforcement under the agreement.
item.dc:creator: Grace Ashford
item.media:description: Masked federal agents have been a frequent presence outside immigration courtrooms in New York City.

item.title: N.Y.C. Schools Could Lose 153,000 Students in Next Decade, Study Finds
item.description: A confluence of factors is causing public schools in New York and across the country to experience dramatic reductions in enrollment.
item.dc:creator: Matthew Haag
item.category: New York City School Construction Authority
item.media:description: New York City’s public schools have lost more than 123,000 students since the Covid-19 pandemic.

item.title: New NTSB Report Into Deadly China Eastern Crash Suggests Struggle in Cockpit
item.description: A report by the U.S. National Transportation Safety Board offers new details about the China Eastern Airlines crash in 2022, which killed all 132 people on board.
item.dc:creator: James Glanz
item.media:description: Emergency workers searching for the black box at the crash site of the China Eastern flight in Teng County, China in 2022.

item.title: The Sun Sets on ‘Jersey Shore.’ For Now, Anyway.
item.description: The new season of “Jersey Shore: Family Vacation” will be the last on MTV. The castmates said it’s only a matter of time before it returns somehow.
item.dc:creator: Dave Itzkoff and Daniel Arnold
item.media:credit: Daniel Arnold for The New York Times
item.media:description: From left: Pauly D, Deena Cortese, Vinny Guadagnino, Jenni Farley, Nicole Polizzi, Mike “The Situation” Sorrentino, Sammi Giancola and Angela Pivarnick.

item.title: America the Undammed
item.description: More miles of the country’s rivers were reconnected last year thanks to dam removals than at any other time in history.
item.dc:creator: Cara Buckley
item.media:credit: Andrew Mangum for The New York Times

item.title: Is the Blake Lively v. Justin Baldoni Legal Fight Over? Not So Fast.
item.description: Lawyers for Ms. Lively and Mr. Baldoni have said the two sides settled much of the contentious dispute. But one major disagreement remains.
item.dc:creator: Julia Jacobs
item.media:description: Despite the joint statement they put out, Blake Lively and Justin Baldoni have not settled a major issue that divides them: Is she due damages in a related legal matter?

item.title: Giuliani Seeks to Have Health Care Covered by 9/11 Program
item.description: The former New York City mayor, who is improving after a serious case of pneumonia, suffers from a respiratory condition linked to his exposure to toxins at ground zero, his spokesman says.
item.dc:creator: Joseph Goldstein and Jonah E. Bromwich
item.media:description: Rudolph W. Giuliani, the former New York City mayor, attended the ceremony commemorating the 24th anniversary of the Sept. 11, 2001, terrorist attacks.

item.title: Why So Few Babies? We Might Have Overlooked the Biggest Reason of All.
item.description: We can’t be certain — and that’s the point.
item.dc:creator: Anna Louie Sussman
item.media:credit: Em Wall for The New York Times

item.title: A Legendary Investor on How to Prevent America’s Coming ‘Heart Attack’
item.description: Why Ray Dalio is bearish on the U.S.
item.dc:creator: Ross Douthat, Emily Holzknecht and Sophia Alvarez Boyd

item.title: Sticker Shock
item.description: We explore the rise in gas prices.
item.dc:creator: Sam Sifton
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

item.title: Late Night Isn’t Buying Trump’s New ‘Skirmish’ in Iran
item.description: After Trump used the terms “miniwar” and “excursion” to describe the conflict in Iran, Jimmy Kimmel predicted that the president would next call the war “a tiff. It will be a $200 billion tiff.”
item.dc:creator: Trish Bendix
item.category: The Daily Show with Jon Stewart (TV Program)
Late Show with Stephen Colbert (TV Program)

item.title: China Sentences 2 Former Defense Ministers on Bribery Charges
item.description: Gen. Wei Fenghe and Gen. Li Shangfu are likely to spend the rest of their lives in prison after receiving suspended death sentences.
item.dc:creator: Chris Buckley
item.media:credit: Roslan Rahman/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images; Caroline Chia/Reuters

item.title: Russia Ramps Up Threats Toward Ukraine Over Victory Day Parade
item.description: Kremlin officials said a large strike on Kyiv would be “inevitable” if Ukraine disrupted the May 9 event, a prospect President Volodymyr Zelensky has appeared to allude to.
item.dc:creator: Cassandra Vinograd and Ivan Nechepurenko
item.media:description: Russia has enhanced security in Moscow ahead of Saturday’s Victory Day parade.

item.title: Mississippi Storm Injures at Least 17 and Damages More Than 1,000 Buildings
item.description: State officials were assessing the damage on Thursday from a tornado cluster and storm that ripped through the region overnight and closed dozens of roads in several counties.
item.dc:creator: Jin Yu Young and Jacey Fortin
item.media:description: A road blocked off in Lincoln County, Miss., after storms moved through the area on Wednesday.

item.title: In Hungary, Voters Exposed the Limits of China’s Ties to Orban
item.description: Beijing depended on Hungary’s outgoing leader, Viktor Orban, to gain a toehold in Europe. A giant battery factory proved a step too far.
item.dc:creator: Andrew Higgins and Lili Rutai
item.media:credit: Akos Stiller for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Contemporary Amperex Technology Ltd, or CATL, battery plant in Debrecen, Hungary, last week. Opposition to the plant soured local voters on Prime Minister Viktor Orban in elections in April.
